reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
bond_formed: C–O and C–C bonds in isoxazole ring
bond_broken: N–O bond rearrangement in nitrile oxide generation; loss of H2O from ethyl nitroacetate

reagents: ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv) in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
feed_streams.reasoning: Both substrates dissolved in DES as a single feed; premixing ensures nitrile oxide is immediately trapped by phenylacetylene dipolarophile, minimizing dimerization to furoxan

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates nitrile oxide in situ via thermal dehydration; 2 equiv used to drive conversion

reagents.name: tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB)
reagents.role: deep eutectic solvent component (hydrogen bond acceptor)
reagents.notes: Forms DES with ethylene glycol; acts as reaction medium and may promote nitrile oxide formation

reagents.name: ethylene glycol (EG)
reagents.role: deep eutectic solvent component (hydrogen bond donor)

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal tautomerization/dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (nitrile oxide intermediate) in situ, facilitated by the DES medium at 120°C
Regioselective [3+2] cycloaddition of the nitrile oxide 1,3-dipole with phenylacetylene (dipolarophile) to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate via concerted pericyclic mechanism

stream_logic.reasoning: Single combined feed: both substrates in DES. Premixing is essential so that the nitrile oxide generated thermally is immediately captured by the dipolarophile, suppressing nitrile oxide dimerization (furoxan byproduct). No catalyst or base needed, so no incompatibility issues with a single stream.

mixing_order_reasoning: Both substrates must be present together when heated. The nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and reactive — if generated without the dipolarophile present, it dimerizes to furoxan. A single premixed feed entering a heated coil reactor ensures immediate trapping. The DES has high viscosity (~50 cP at RT) but becomes much less viscous at 120°C, so laminar flow in a heated coil is acceptable.
deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species; reaction proceeds under air in batch protocol.
quench_reasoning: Batch protocol quenches with water for workup purposes only; in flow, the product stream can be collected directly and worked up offline. No reactive intermediates persist after the short residence time at 120°C.
wavelength_reasoning: Purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation involved.
confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism: nitrile oxide generation from nitroacetate is well-established. The exact role of DES in promoting nitrile oxide formation (H-bond activation, polarity effects) is not fully characterized but does not affect flow design. Viscosity of TBAB/EG DES at 120°C is estimated (~5-10 cP); actual value should be measured for precise flow rate/pressure drop calculations. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate suggests some decomposition/dimerization of the nitrile oxide, which flow's precise temperature control may reduce, potentially allowing lower excess. Single-stream approach assumes substrates are stable together at room temperature in DES before entering the heated zone — this is consistent with the batch protocol where both are added to DES before heating.

intensification_mandate.flow_justification_basis: Flow provides (1) rapid, uniform heating to 120°C via high surface-to-volume ratio in narrow-bore tubing (e.g., 1 mm ID stainless steel coil), enabling superheating of DES above its batch boiling point under back-pressure, potentially reducing residence time from 15 min to ~5 min; (2) precise residence time control minimizes over-reaction and furoxan byproduct formation from nitrile oxide dimerization; (3) continuous operation with DES recycling improves throughput; (4) back-pressure regulation (e.g., 10-15 bar) allows safe operation above atmospheric boiling point of the solvent mixture, further accelerating the reaction. The viscous DES (~50 cP at RT, ~5-10 cP at 120°C) flows well in laminar regime through 1 mm ID tubing.
